calculatorHTML:
Recipe Scaling Calculator

Scaled Ingredients

howItWorks.intro: Scaling a recipe means adjusting all ingredient amounts proportionally so the dish comes out the same, just in a different quantity.
rules: Calculate the scaling factor: desired servings ÷ original servings
Multiply each ingredient amount by the scaling factor
Results display as smart fractions (e.g., 1½ instead of 1.5)
howItWorks.outro: This calculator handles the math automatically and converts decimal results to easy-to-read fractions. Note that cooking times and pan sizes may need adjustment when significantly scaling up or down.

faq.question: How do I double a recipe?
faq.answer: Set the original servings to whatever the recipe makes, then set desired servings to twice that number. For example, if a recipe serves 4, enter 4 as original and 8 as desired. Every ingredient amount will be doubled.

faq.question: How do I halve a recipe?
faq.answer: Enter the original servings, then enter half that number as desired servings. The calculator will divide each ingredient amount by two and display the result in easy-to-read fractions.

faq.question: Do I need to adjust cooking time when scaling?
faq.answer: For small changes (doubling or halving), cooking time usually stays about the same if you use a larger or smaller pan. For very large batches, you may need to increase time slightly. Always check for doneness rather than relying solely on time.

faq.question: What about eggs when scaling recipes?
faq.answer: Eggs are tricky to scale. If scaling results in a fraction like 1.5 eggs, round to the nearest whole number (2 eggs). For recipes where egg count is critical (like soufflés), it's better to use a scale and weigh the egg, then use the proportional weight.

faq.question: Should I adjust seasonings linearly?
faq.answer: Salt, spices, and strong flavors like garlic don't always scale linearly. When doubling, start with 1.5x the seasoning and adjust to taste. When scaling up by 3x or more, use about 2x the seasoning and taste as you go.

faq.question: Can I scale baking recipes the same way?
faq.answer: Baking recipes can be scaled, but be careful with leavening agents (baking powder, baking soda, yeast). These don't always scale linearly. When doubling, use 1.5x to 1.75x the leavening. Also adjust pan size and baking time accordingly.

sections.title: What Is the Recipe Scaling Calculator?
sections.body: The Recipe Scaling Calculator is a free online tool designed for home cooks and chefs who need quick, accurate calculations in the cooking and food preparation space. By entering your original recipe servings, desired servings, ingredients, you get instant results. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting these numbers right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In cooking and food preparation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by home cooks and chefs worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to perfect your recipes with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your original recipe servings and need to find the right result. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.
